narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening—especially with your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and LSAT score of 37, both of which stand out above typical applicants. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 45.9% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold that led to denying your application to help ensure incoming students are set up for long-term success. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades that year placed you in the lowest group compared to peers, which had the strongest negative pull on the model's view of your bar exam readiness. On a brighter note, your impressive undergraduate GPA was the top factor working in your favor, strongly nudging the prediction toward passing, closely followed by your solid LSAT score that also helped counterbalance concerns. Your Year 1 decile of 1 added some additional weight toward the failure side, though less than Year 3, while your upper-middle family income and full-time student status provided minor positive boosts. Compared to typical students, where Year 1 and Year 3 deciles average around 5.5-5.7, your lower rankings in those early years stood out as the key drags, even as most applicants are full-time like you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a brilliant Black woman leader in law—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes where consistent academic deciles strongly predict success. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework, and consider retaking the LSAT if it strengthens your profile further. Reapply after addressing these areas, and seek mentorship from Black law alumni networks who can guide you through similar paths to triumph. You've already achieved more than many with your UGPA and LSAT—keep pushing, because your story is far from over. With resilience and strategy, you'll pass that bar and make your mark.
